title: Chapter 18: The Queen's Choice
content: The next three days were a blur of preparation.

Magnus worked day and night to translate the ancient symbols on the seal chamber walls. Darius drilled the pack's warriors in combat formations designed to fight something that wasn't a wolf, wasn't a rogue, wasn't anything they'd ever faced before. The servants stockpiled supplies. The healers prepared for casualties.
And Sera trained.

She trained harder than she'd ever trained — not just her wolf form, but her mind-link, her connection to the Goddess, her understanding of the ancient magic that was her birthright. Every morning, she meditated in the Moon Chamber, reaching for the silver light that pulsed through her birthmark. Every afternoon, she sparred with Kael, pushing her wolf to its limits. Every evening, she studied with Magnus, learning everything the old wolf could teach her about the Moon-Marked and their ancient war against the Shadow.
"You're pushing yourself too hard," Kael said on the third evening, finding her in the Moon Chamber long after midnight.

"The seal is getting weaker. I can feel it — even from here. Every hour, the cracks spread a little more. Every hour, the Shadow gets a little stronger." She didn't look up from the scroll she was studying. "I don't have time to rest."
"You don't have time to collapse from exhaustion, either."
Kael crossed the chamber and knelt beside her. His hand covered hers, stilling her frantic note-taking.
"Sera. Look at me."
She looked. His golden eyes were soft — not the cold Alpha gaze she'd first seen in the trial, but something warmer. Something that had grown over weeks of fighting and trusting and loving.
"You're afraid," he said. "I can feel it through the bond. You're terrified that you're not strong enough. That the prophecy was wrong. That the Shadow will break free and destroy everything."

"I'm terrified," she admitted. "Because Aldric was right about one thing. The first Moon-Marked died fighting the Shadow. She was stronger than me — older, more experienced, probably had years of training. And she still had to sacrifice herself to seal it. What chance do I have?"
"You have me. You have the pack. You have something the first Moon-Marked didn't have." Kael lifted her hand to his lips. "You have a reason to survive."
"The first Moon-Marked had a reason to survive too. She had a pack. A family. Probably people she loved."
"But did she have a prophecy telling her she would win?"
Sera was silent.

"The prophecy doesn't say you'll die," Kael said. "It says you'll prevail. It says the Silver Queen and the Alpha of Two Hearts will face the darkness together. It doesn't say 'and then the Queen will sacrifice herself and everyone will be very sad.'" His lips curved. "The Goddess has a flair for the dramatic, but she's not cruel."
"How do you know?"

"Because she chose you." His voice was steady. "Because she gave you a mind-link so you could hear my ridiculous inner wolf and fall in love with me instead of running away. Because she arranged the binding ritual so we'd be strong enough to face this together. Everything the Goddess has done — the link, the prophecy, the trial, the bond — has been leading to this moment. She wouldn't lead us here just to let us fail."

*"Kael is RIGHT. The Goddess is a MATCHMAKER, not a VILLAIN. She wants us to WIN. She wants us to be HAPPY. She wants us to get MARRIED and have SILVER-BLACK WOLF PUPS and rule the pack for a THOUSAND YEARS. Okay, maybe not the thousand years part. But definitely the married part. DEFINITELY the pups part."*
Sera laughed — a surprised, watery sound. "Fenris is planning our future children."
"Of course he is." Kael pulled her to her feet. "Come on. You need to sleep. Tomorrow, we'll figure out how to beat an ancient shadow monster. Tonight, you rest."
"Kael — "
"That's an order. From your Alpha." He paused. "Also from the man who loves you and doesn't want to watch you collapse from exhaustion."

She let him lead her out of the Moon Chamber, through the dark corridors of the castle, to his chambers — their chambers now. The bed was warm and familiar. His arms were steady around her. And for the first time in three days, Sera let herself sleep.
She dreamed of the Moon Goddess.

The dream was vivid — more vivid than any dream should be. She was standing in a field of silver grass under a sky full of stars, and before her was a woman made of moonlight. Her features shifted constantly — one moment young, the next ancient, the next neither and both.
"Seraphina Vale." The Goddess's voice was like wind chimes and wolf howls woven together. "You've come a long way since the trial."
"You did this," Sera said. "The mind-link. The prophecy. All of it."
"I nudged. You did the rest." The Goddess smiled — a shifting, luminous expression. "I don't control destiny, child. I just... arrange the pieces. What you do with them is up to you."
"The Shadow. How do I defeat it?"

"The first Moon-Marked sealed it because she couldn't destroy it. She wasn't strong enough. But you..." The Goddess's silver eyes met hers. "You're not just Moon-Marked anymore. You're bound. The Alpha's power flows through you now, and yours through him. Together, you might be strong enough to do what she couldn't."
"Might be?"

"Prophecies are not guarantees. They are possibilities. The one where you win is the one I believe in — but you have to believe in it too." The Goddess reached out and touched Sera's birthmark. "The seal will break at the next dark moon. That gives you twenty-five days to prepare. Use them well."
"And if we're not ready?"
"Then the Shadow rises, and the pack — and everything beyond it — falls." The Goddess's expression softened. "But you will be ready. I chose you for a reason, Seraphina. Don't forget that."
Sera woke with a gasp.

Kael was still asleep beside her, his arm draped across her waist. The pre-dawn light was grey outside the window. Twenty-five days until the next dark moon. Twenty-five days to prepare for the battle that would determine everything.
She pressed a kiss to Kael's forehead — he stirred but didn't wake — and slipped out of bed.
It was time to get to work.
The pack council met at dawn.

"We have twenty-five days," Sera announced, standing at the head of the table with Kael beside her. "The seal in the old tombs is failing. On the next dark moon, the Shadow will break free unless we stop it."
"Can we reinforce the seal?" one of the nobles asked. "Channel more power into it?"

"I'm working on it. Magnus is translating the ancient symbols — there might be a ritual that can strengthen the seal temporarily. But it's a stopgap, not a solution." Sera met the noble's eyes. "The only permanent solution is to destroy the Shadow."
"Destroy an ancient entity that's existed for thousands of years? How?"

"I don't know yet. But I know we need to be ready for anything." She spread a map of the Blackwood across the table. "The seal chamber is here. When the Shadow breaks free — whether that's in twenty-five days or later — it will emerge into the forest here. We need to be waiting."
"We'll fortify this area," Darius said, pointing to a ridge above the tomb entrance. "Archers here. Heavy fighters here. A fallback line here."
"The rogues were vulnerable to silver," Sera added. "The Shadow might be too. We should stockpile silver weapons — arrowheads, blades, anything we can forge in three weeks."
"I'll put the smiths on it immediately."

Kael stepped forward. "This is not just a battle. This is the fight the prophecy was written for. The Silver Queen and the Alpha of Two Hearts, facing the darkness that has threatened our pack for a thousand years. Every wolf who fights in this battle will be remembered. Every wolf who stands with us will be honored." His golden eyes swept the council. "I won't order anyone to fight. The Shadow is... beyond anything we've faced. If you choose to run — to take your families and flee — I won't stop you. But I hope you'll stay."
The silence stretched.
Then Darius stood. "I've followed you through poisonings and rogue attacks and a madman's army. I'm not about to stop now."
One by one, the other wolves rose. The warriors. The nobles. Even a few of the servants who had been allowed into the council at Sera's insistence. Every wolf in the room stood.
"The pack stands together," Magnus said quietly. "As it has for a thousand years. As it will for a thousand more."
Sera looked at the assembled wolves — wolves who had once feared her, despised her, tried to destroy her. Now they were standing. For her. With her.
She didn't cry.
But it was a near thing.
"Twenty-five days," she said. "Let's make them count."
